# Changelog

## [Unreleased]

### Fixed

- **A deeply nested payload made a log call throw `RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded` back at the application, and made the request middleware drop the entire HTTP entry** (`src/redact.ts`, `src/logger.ts`, `src/request-middleware.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`, `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts`): `redactValue` is plain recursion with no depth bound, and winston runs its formats **synchronously** inside `logger.log()` with nothing wrapping the call — so the overflow surfaced as an exception thrown by the application's own `logger.info(...)`. It overflowed at roughly **half** the nesting depth `JSON.stringify` itself tolerates (measured on V8: `RangeError` at depth 2000, while `JSON.stringify` was still fine at 4000), which meant merely enabling `maskMetaKeys` turned a working log call into a crash for a payload — ~18KB of JSON, trivially reachable by logging a parsed request body — that the same logger rendered fine without redaction. Reproduced in both the pretty (`formatMessage`) and json (`buildMetaRedactor`) pipelines. In the middleware the same root cause had a quieter symptom: `serializeBody` runs the walk over `req.body`, `finalize()`'s catch swallowed the `RangeError`, and **zero** log entries were emitted for that request — not even the method, url, and status, none of which ever touched the body — giving an attacker a cheap way to make their own requests structurally invisible in the HTTP log by posting a deep body (well under `express.json()`'s 100kb default). The walk is now bounded at `MAX_REDACT_DEPTH` (256, exported from `src/redact.ts`): an object or array nested deeper is replaced with the sentinel `"[MaxDepth]"`. The bound sits far above any realistic log payload and far below the engine's frame limit, and the check runs after the primitive fast-path, so a scalar leaf at any depth still renders and only a value that would itself recurse is traded for the sentinel. The existing 3-arg and 4-arg (`forceCopy`) call signatures are unchanged. `finalize()` additionally wraps its `serializeBody` call so a body that cannot be serialized degrades to a `"[UNSERIALIZABLE]"` body while the rest of the entry is still logged; the outer catch is retained as the defense-in-depth net it already was for `enrich` / `messageBuilder` / `logger.log`.

  The plan for this fix also called for skipping the walk entirely when no `maskBodyKeys` and no `redactPaths` are configured, on the premise that it is "a pure no-op deep copy" there. That premise is false and the change was **not** made: with an empty mask set the walk still performs three load-bearing jobs — it renders cycles as `"[Circular]"` (a body that would otherwise collapse to the useless `"[object Object]"` via the `String()` fallback), it strips own `__proto__` / `constructor` / `prototype` keys (making prototype-pollution hardening unconditional rather than contingent on having configured a mask), and it is the only thing making `entry.requestBody` structurally independent of the live `req.body`, which `bodySnapshot` deliberately does not copy. The depth bound removes the crash risk that motivated the skip, and the default configuration never calls `serializeBody` at all (`includeRequestBody` is opt-in), so the skip would have traded three real guarantees for an optimization the default path does not need.

- **The pretty-mode formatter could throw at the caller on input that needed no redaction at all** (`src/logger.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`): three `JSON.stringify` calls in the printf — the non-string `message` branch, the non-string `stack` branch, and the metadata block — ran unguarded inside the synchronous format pipeline, so their exceptions propagated out of the application's `logger.info(...)` rather than degrading the line. Two reachable inputs crashed the **default, no-mask** configuration: a payload nested deeply enough to exhaust `JSON.stringify` itself (`RangeError`), and a self-referencing value on `info.message` — `logger.info(obj)` where `obj.self === obj` — which threw `TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON`. The first case produced a perverse inversion: with `maskMetaKeys` configured the redaction walk bounds the graph before the metadata stringify sees it, so **enabling** redaction was safer than leaving it off. All three sites now route through a `safeStringify` helper that degrades to `"[UNSERIALIZABLE]"`, so the pretty-mode line always renders rather than propagating a throw. The boundary is deliberate and narrow: this makes the pretty-mode **formatter** total, not `JSON.stringify` — a value no JSON serializer can express renders as the sentinel rather than as data. **Known remaining boundary:** `format: "json"` with no `maskMetaKeys` configured can still throw `RangeError` on a payload deep enough (~8000) to exhaust `JSON.stringify` itself, from winston's own `format.json()` serializer — i.e. only where the data is genuinely un-JSON-able, matching bare winston. Configuring `maskMetaKeys` makes even that unreachable, since the redaction walk bounds the graph to 256 first.

- **A metadata value whose getter threw crashed the log call, and could have leaked on failure** (`src/logger.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`): the redaction walk reads own enumerable keys, and reading a key invokes a getter — caller code, which may throw (as may a hostile `toJSON`). Both redaction sites now **fail closed**: `formatMessage` collapses the metadata block to `{ _redactionFailed: true }` and `buildMetaRedactor` sets the offending key to `"[RedactionFailed]"`, in both cases substituting a marker rather than falling back to the raw value — emitting the unredacted value would turn a redaction failure into a secret disclosure, precisely the outcome `maskMetaKeys` exists to prevent, and would do so exactly when an exotic or adversarial object is in the payload. `buildMetaRedactor` degrades only the offending key, so one hostile value cannot suppress masking for the rest of the line, and it swaps in a fresh cycle-tracking `WeakSet` on failure: the throw unwinds past the `seen.delete(...)` each branch performs on its way out, leaving the abandoned subtree's objects marked "on the active path", so reusing that set would misreport a later key legitimately referencing one of them as `"[Circular]"` and silently drop its data.

- **A shut-down logger stayed in the cache, so the next `createLogger()` handed back the ended instance and silently discarded every line** (`src/logger.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`): `shutdownLogger()` deregistered crash capture and released its shared global-file handle but never evicted `loggerRegistry`, so a later `createLogger()` for the same `moduleName` + `logDirectory` cache-hit and returned the **same, ended** logger — `b === a`, `b.transports.length === 0`. Nothing surfaced the problem: winston derives `transports` from the readable's pipe targets and Node auto-unpipes each transport as it finishes; its own "Attempt to write logs with no transports" guard is dead code on modern Node (it tests `!this._readableState.pipes`, and `pipes` is an empty **array** — truthy); and the resulting write-after-end error is swallowed by the base logger's no-op `error` listener. So `b.info(...)` threw nothing, warned nothing, and wrote nothing. The stale entry also returned **before** `registerCrashCapture`, leaving process-wide crash capture permanently dead after the first shutdown. Worker recycles, dev hot-reloads, aborted shutdowns and shutdown-then-recreate test loops all reached it. The registry slot is now evicted through a `WeakMap<winston.Logger, string>` recorded alongside the existing Proxy→base mapping (an O(1) delete rather than a registry scan), and only when the slot still points at that exact logger — the WeakMap can outlive the slot it names, and after a `resetLoggerRegistry()` the key is re-claimed by a different, live logger whose entry must survive its detached predecessor's teardown. Eviction happens **synchronously, in the same tick as `end()`, on the timeout path as well as the success path**: `end()` is issued unconditionally before the flush race starts, so evicting only on success would both leave a window as wide as `timeoutMs` (5000ms by default) in which a concurrent `createLogger()` still receives the ended logger, and preserve the defect outright whenever a flush overran its deadline (a timed-out logger is ended *and* undrained — strictly more broken, not less). Retrying a timed-out shutdown through the reference you hold is unaffected, since `shutdownLogger` never reads the registry; the one accepted trade-off is that `shutdownAllLoggers()`, which iterates the registry, will not re-attempt a timed-out logger on a second bulk call. `logger.close()` and `logger.end()` evict too — both are terminal and leave a zero-transport logger, so they were the same silent-loss bug behind a different door. Verified end-to-end against the built bundle: a post-shutdown `createLogger()` now returns a distinct, working logger whose line is readable from disk, and crash capture re-registers.

- **`shutdownLogger()` / `shutdownAllLoggers()` resolved with nothing on disk, so the documented SIGTERM idiom lost every buffered line** (`src/logger.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`): `DailyRotateFile` defines no `_final`, so Node emits its `finish` as soon as the queued `_write` calls return — while the underlying `logStream` is still buffering. Everything that trusted that `finish` inherited the lie, including winston's own `Logger._final` (which awaits each transport's `finish` after calling `end()`) and therefore `shutdownLogger()`, whose contract is to resolve "once every transport has flushed". The result was total, silent data loss for the exact shutdown sequence the `shutdownAllLoggers` JSDoc and `README.md` prescribe: `await shutdownAllLoggers(); process.exit(0)` — measured at **0 of 2000 lines** written, 3/3 runs; a single line before `await shutdownLogger(...)` never even created the file. This is the same root cause v1.0.0 fixed on the crash-exit path (where `settleTransport` drains via `close()` instead); the graceful-shutdown path never got the fix. Rather than teach each caller to prefer `close()`, the rotating transport is now given the `Writable._final` hook it omits, draining `logStream` exactly the way its own `close()` already proves is correct — so `end()` and `close()` finally agree and every consumer of `finish` (`shutdownLogger`, `logger.end()`, winston itself) becomes truthful at once. Verified end-to-end against the built bundle: **2000 of 2000** lines on disk after `await shutdownAllLoggers(); process.exit(0)`, plus a single line, the default module + shared-global-file config, `includeGlobalFile: false`, and a partial shutdown where one logger releases the shared global file while another keeps writing to it. A caller-supplied transport that brings its own `_final` (`winston.transports.File`) is never clobbered, and `shutdownLogger` still calls `logger.end()` — which is what hands over the backlog a back-pressuring async transport leaves in the Logger's own buffers.

## [1.0.0] - 2026-07-16

This release removes the `MaxListenersExceededWarning` that appeared in any application creating a logger per module, and — because fixing it correctly meant taking ownership of crash capture — settles three long-standing behaviors that could not be changed without a major version. See **Migration** in `README.md` for the upgrade path.

### Fixed

- **Every `createLogger()` call installed its own `uncaughtException` + `unhandledRejection` listener on `process`, so an app with ~11 module loggers tripped Node's `MaxListenersExceededWarning`** (`src/logger.ts`, `src/crash-capture.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`): `captureUncaught` defaulted to `true` and set `handleExceptions: true` / `handleRejections: true` on the file transports. Winston's `Logger.add(transport)` reacts to those flags by calling `this.exceptions.handle()` / `this.rejections.handle()` (`winston/lib/winston/logger.js`), and `handle()` installs a process listener guarded by `if (!this.catcher)` — a guard that lives on the **per-logger** handler instance. Extra transports on the _same_ logger were therefore free, but every _new_ logger added a fresh pair, and each distinct `moduleName` is a distinct registry key and thus a brand-new winston logger. Eleven modules produced exactly 11 + 11 listeners against Node's default limit of 10, reproducing the reported warning verbatim. Fixed by removing the `handleExceptions` / `handleRejections` flags from every transport (Console, module file, global file, and each `additionalTransports` entry — the `exceptionHandlerTarget` election is gone) so winston installs **zero** process listeners, and introducing a process-wide coordinator (`src/crash-capture.ts`) that owns exactly **one** listener pair for the whole process no matter how many loggers exist. Capture-enabled loggers register with the coordinator and deregister on `shutdownLogger()` / `resetLoggerRegistry()`, so a process that shuts every logger down returns to **zero** package-owned process listeners. Verified end-to-end against the built ESM and CJS bundles: 11 module loggers now yield 1 + 1 listeners and no warning. A winston canary test asserts `logger.exceptions.catcher` stays falsy and `logger.exceptions.handlers.size === 0`, so a future winston change that reintroduces a per-logger catcher fails loudly instead of silently.
- **`shutdownLogger()` and `resetLoggerRegistry()` orphaned process listeners forever** (`src/logger.ts`): `shutdownLogger()` uses `logger.end()`, which — unlike `logger.close()` — does **not** call `exceptions.unhandle()` / `rejections.unhandle()`, so winston's per-logger listeners survived a graceful shutdown. Worse, the still-installed catcher would then call `logger.log(...)` on an ended writable and the resulting stream error was swallowed by the no-op `baseLogger.on("error")` handler, silently dropping the very crash trace `captureUncaught: true` promises. `resetLoggerRegistry()` had the same problem: it cleared the cache but left every detached logger's listeners installed. Both now deregister from the coordinator, and the listener pair is uninstalled once the last capture-enabled logger is gone.
- **A single uncaught exception was written once per logger into the shared global log** (`src/crash-capture.ts`): because all N loggers handled the event and all N write to the same `all-logs-%DATE%.log`, that one file received N identical stack traces (and the console N copies). The crash is now recorded exactly **once**, through a single elected logger.
- **N loggers opened N `DailyRotateFile` handles against the same `all-logs-%DATE%.log`** (`src/shared-file-transport.ts`, `src/logger.ts`): every logger with `includeGlobalFile: true` built its own rotating-file transport for one shared path, producing N file handles and N independent rotation state machines racing to rotate, size-check, and gzip the same file. The underlying transport is now created once per resolved path and **reference-counted**; each logger is piped a cheap forwarding handle instead. Naively sharing the transport itself would have been unsafe — winston's `Logger._final` calls `transport.end()` on every piped transport, so one `shutdownLogger()` would have ended the shared transport and silently destroyed global-file logging for every other module — which the handle indirection prevents. Per-module labels and per-logger `level` gating are preserved, the shared transport's `error` events fan out to every sharing logger through a **single** listener (one listener per logger would have recreated a `MaxListenersExceededWarning` on the transport itself), and it is closed only when the last handle releases.

- **The crash-exit path could lose the very crash record it exists to write** (`src/crash-capture.ts`, `src/shared-file-transport.ts`): `DailyRotateFile` defines no `_final`, so `end()` runs straight through readable-stream's prefinish and emits `finish` on the next tick **while the underlying `logStream` is still buffering** — measured: `end()` reports `finish` with **0 bytes** on disk, `close()` reports it with the bytes written. Both the shared-transport teardown and the exit flush awaited `end()`'s `finish` and then called `process.exit(1)`, racing the write. Compounding it, shared global files are refcounted, so ending only the crashing logger's handle left the shared `all-logs` file open and un-flushed whenever any other logger still held a reference — the normal case in the one-logger-per-module setup this package targets. Both paths now drain via `close()` (the real flush), and the exit path additionally calls `flushSharedFileTransportsForExit()`, which closes every shared transport regardless of refcount (correct precisely because `process.exit(1)` is about to take every logger down). Teardown is memoised per entry so the last release and the exit flush cannot race two `close()` calls. Verified deterministic across repeated runs: 11 sharing loggers, crash written once, present on disk, exit code 1.
- **An unref'd exit timer could turn a fatal into exit code `0`** (`src/crash-capture.ts`): the flush timeout was `unref()`'d, so if transports never settled and nothing else held the event loop open, Node would fall off the loop and exit **0** — reporting success for a process that died of an uncaught exception — in exactly the stuck-transport case the timeout exists for. The timer is now ref'd (matching winston's own equivalent) and cleared as soon as the flush race settles.
- **Releasing a shared transport removed its only `error` listener** (`src/shared-file-transport.ts`): the shared transport is never piped into a logger, so winston never attaches its own `error` handler and the fan-out listener is the sole one. Detaching it on release left an EventEmitter with zero `error` listeners while `DailyRotateFile` can still emit asynchronously (log pruning, and the whole `zippedArchive` gzip pipeline) — turning a post-shutdown gzip failure into an `ERR_UNHANDLED_ERROR` that takes the process down, where previously it was a `console.error` line. The listener is now retained and reports through the bare console once no handles remain.
- **A console-only logger could win the crash-capture election and keep the trace off disk** (`src/crash-capture.ts`): with de-duplication only the elected logger records a crash, so if an app's first capture-enabled logger had no file transport (a bootstrap/console-only logger), the trace never reached disk even when file-backed loggers existed — breaking the "persisted across restarts" promise that pre-v1.0.0 kept by routing winston's exception flags to file transports ahead of the console. Election now prefers the first registered logger with a file transport, then any logger with at least one transport (a transport-less logger can only produce winston's "Attempt to write logs with no transports" warning), then the first.
- **`logger.close()` no longer leaves a closed logger capturing crashes** (`src/logger.ts`): winston's own `Logger.close()` calls `exceptions.unhandle()` / `rejections.unhandle()`, so before v1.0.0 closing a logger inherently stopped its crash capture. With capture coordinated by this package instead of winston, a closed-but-still-registered logger could remain the elected primary; the coordinator would then route the next crash into its ended stream, winston would drop the write ("Attempt to write logs with no transports"), and the no-op `error` listener would swallow the failure — losing the crash **entirely and silently**, even when other healthy loggers existed. The public logger Proxy now intercepts `close()` to deregister from the coordinator first, preserving the pre-v1.0.0 semantics and handing the primary role to a logger that can still write.
- **A caller-supplied `additionalTransports` entry carrying `handleExceptions` / `handleRejections` re-created the per-logger listener leak** (`src/logger.ts`): the flags are what drive winston's `Logger.add()` to install a process listener for that logger, so a pre-flagged custom transport reintroduced the original defect (one pair per logger, warning past ~10) and additionally logged every crash twice — once via winston's own catcher writing to that transport, once via the coordinator. `createLogger()` now clears both flags from every caller-supplied transport and warns once per process explaining why. Verified: five loggers each given a pre-flagged transport now add 1 listener in total, not 6. **This does change where crashes land, and the warning says so:** because a crash is recorded once through the elected primary, a flagged transport receives crashes only if it belongs to that logger — a dedicated crash sink attached to a non-primary module logger stops receiving them and should be moved to the primary. See item 5 of the migration table in `README.md`.

### Added

- **`LoggerOptions.exitOnUncaught?: boolean` (default `true`)** (`src/types.ts`, `src/logger.ts`, `src/crash-capture.ts`): controls whether a captured fatal terminates the process with exit code `1` after the crash is logged and flushed (bounded by an internal 3000 ms budget so a stuck transport cannot wedge the exit). Governs **both** `uncaughtException` and `unhandledRejection`. Set to `false` for the pre-1.0.0 "log and keep running" behavior. Included in the registry options signature, so a divergent value on a cached key raises the existing one-time conflict warning.

### Changed

- **BREAKING — a captured fatal now exits the process with code `1` by default.** Previously the underlying winston logger was always created with `exitOnError: false` and no exit behavior was installed, so merely constructing a logger silently suppressed Node's default crash-on-uncaught semantics: the error was logged and the application limped on in a potentially inconsistent state. The default is now to restore Node's standard behavior. Set `exitOnUncaught: false` to keep the old behavior.
- **BREAKING — a crash is logged once, not once per logger.** The elected primary is the first still-registered capture-enabled logger, so the crash lands in that module's file plus the shared global file plus its console; other modules' files no longer each receive a copy. Because capture no longer routes through winston's `handleExceptions` transports, the crash is written to **all** of the primary's transports (the console included, where previously a file-enabled logger sent crashes only to files).
- **BREAKING — the crash payload's `exception: true` / `rejection: true` marker is replaced by `crash: "uncaughtException" | "unhandledRejection"`.** Those two fields are winston-reserved routing flags: `winston-transport`'s `_accept` / `_write` **drop** an `{ exception: true }` info from any transport lacking `handleExceptions`, which this package now sets on no transport — so keeping the marker would have made the crash silently vanish from every transport. Everything else winston's `getAllInfo()` produces (the `<kind>: <message>` text, `stack`, parsed `trace`, and the `process` / `os` diagnostic blocks) is preserved unchanged. Consumers grepping for `"exception":true` should switch to `"crash":`.
- **BREAKING — `logger.transports` no longer contains a `DailyRotateFile` for the global log.** The global slot is now the forwarding handle described above; the module-scoped file remains a real `DailyRotateFile`. Code doing `logger.transports.filter(t => t instanceof DailyRotateFile)` will find one entry where it previously found two.
- **`captureUncaught` keeps its name, default (`true`), and intent**, but now means "register this logger with the process-wide crash coordinator" rather than "set winston's per-transport exception flags". `captureUncaught: false` still opts out entirely and now provably adds no process listener.
- **`resetLoggerRegistry()` additionally resets the crash-capture coordinator and drops the shared global-file registry.** Already-created loggers keep working (their shared transport is not closed out from under them, preserving the documented "detached loggers remain valid" contract); they simply no longer participate in crash capture.

### Internal

- New `src/crash-capture.ts` (coordinator; single listener pair, primary election, exit semantics, injectable `process.exit` for tests) and `src/shared-file-transport.ts` (reference-counted shared global-file transport plus the per-logger forwarding handle). The handle is built from `winston-transport`'s `log` / `close` constructor options rather than a subclass, keeping the package's functional style; it is reached via `winston.Transport` rather than importing `winston-transport` directly, because the latter is an undeclared transitive dependency whose CommonJS body would be inlined into the ESM bundle and throw `Dynamic require of "util" is not supported` at import time.
- Test suite grows 383 → 414 across three spec files, with 100% statement/branch/function/line coverage maintained. New coverage includes the process-listener **delta** for 11 distinct-module loggers (asserted as a delta, never an absolute, because jest installs its own process listeners), crash de-duplication, primary re-election after the current primary shuts down, the real listeners installed on `process` (not just the handler behind them), transport sharing / level gating / shutdown safety / last-release-closes / cache-hit refcount correctness, and the coordinator's defensive paths.
- **The release workflow now authenticates to npm with trusted publishing (OIDC) instead of a long-lived token** (`.github/workflows/release.yml`): the `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN` / `secrets.NPM_TOKEN` env block is gone, so no npm credential exists in CI to leak, expire, or rotate. This required moving the job off Node 22's **bundled** npm — trusted publishing needs npm >= 11.5.1, and no Node 22 release ever bundled npm 11.x (the newest, v22.23.1, ships npm 10.9.8, which contains no OIDC exchange code at all), so the job now runs Node 24.x with `check-latest: true` and a guard step that fails loudly if npm ever drops below the 11.5.1 floor rather than letting it degrade into a misleading `E404`/`ENEEDAUTH` at publish time. `registry-url` is deliberately retained (npm's `publish` calls the OIDC exchange before reading stored credentials and overwrites the token it finds, so the `.npmrc` auth line is superseded, not respected) and `--provenance` is deliberately kept explicit (OIDC's automatic provenance depends on a registry visibility lookup wrapped in a catch that only logs at verbose level, so relying on it can publish an unattested release silently).
- **Release workflow hardening** (`.github/workflows/release.yml`): the tag ref reaches the two scripts that consume it through `env:` rather than `${{ }}` interpolation, closing a script-injection path into a job that can mint an npm publish token; `permissions` defaults to none at the workflow level and is elevated per job; every action is pinned to a full commit SHA; `persist-credentials: false` keeps `GITHUB_TOKEN` out of `.git/config`; dependency caching is disabled per npm's release-build guidance so a poisoned cache cannot reach a provenance-signed artifact; the job carries a 15-minute timeout; and the changelog extractor matches section headings literally instead of building a dynamic `awk` regex whose unescaped dots matched any character.

## [0.22.0] - 2026-07-01

### Security

- **Body-scoped `redactPaths` could leak a secret inside a truncated body's `_preview`, silently mutate the caller's live `req.body`, or bloat the log with a built-in's internal state** (`src/request-middleware.ts`, `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts`): `serializeBody` applies `maskBodyKeys` redaction and then, for an over-limit body, collapses the result into the `{ _truncated: true, _originalLength, _preview }` envelope. Three defects converged on the way body-scoped `redactPaths` were applied. (1) **Leak** — the post-assembly `redactPaths` loop in `finalize()` only ran AFTER `serializeBody` returned, by which point an over-limit body no longer had the nested key the path targeted, so a secret targeted ONLY by `redactPaths` (e.g. `["body.password"]`, no matching `maskBodyKeys`) leaked verbatim inside `_preview` whenever the serialized body exceeded `maxBodyLength` (default 3000) — a silent, size-dependent leak requiring only `includeRequestBody: true` + `includeHttpContext: true`; the identical payload under the limit redacted correctly, so it was easy to miss. (2) **Mutation** — applying paths in place could reach and overwrite a caller-owned object: a class body that defines `toJSON()` while exposing its own fields (a common DTO/ORM pattern), an instance with only non-enumerable own keys, or any value `redactValue` passes through by identity was mutated directly, silently overwriting a field on the live `req.body` after the response finished (observable by anything holding a longer-lived reference — caches, audit trails, retry queues, post-response async work); a secret held only in a private field (`#password`, surfaced via `toJSON`) could also reach the log unredacted. (3) **Bloat** — a `moment`/`Date` (or any `toJSON`-defining value that owns many internal fields) sitting incidentally in the body could be rebuilt from those internal fields, replacing a compact ISO timestamp with a multi-KB internal-state dump. Fixed by applying body-scoped `redactPaths` BEFORE the truncation decision, on a fully-owned `toJSON`-resolved deep copy — `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(masked))` — via a throw-away `{ requestBody: owned }` `redactEntryPath` wrapper (reusing its `body.` → `requestBody.` alias and prototype-pollution guards). Operating on the copy (a) removes the secret from the graph before any truncation can flatten it into `_preview`, (b) guarantees the in-place write never touches the caller's object, and (c) — because the round-trip resolves every `toJSON()` exactly as the final log serializer does — renders an incidental built-in via its compact `toJSON()` output (a `moment`/`Date` → its ISO string, not its internal fields) and redacts (rather than leaks) a secret exposed only through `toJSON()`. A body that cannot be JSON-serialized (e.g. one carrying a `BigInt`) is caught and degrades to the existing `String()` truncation fallback. `finalize()` passes the resolved `redactPaths` into `serializeBody`'s 4th `bodyRedactPaths` parameter; the post-assembly `redactPaths` loop is unchanged and remains the only place `context.*` / `requestHeaders.*` / `responseHeaders.*` paths are redacted (re-applying a body path there is an idempotent no-op). New tests assert: an over-limit body with `redactPaths: ["body.password"]` and no `maskBodyKeys` produces a `_preview` that omits the secret and contains `"[REDACTED]"`; a small (non-truncated) body still redacts; direct `serializeBody(body, undefined, maxLength, ["body.password"])` closes the over-limit leak while omitting the 4th argument preserves the exact 3-arg behavior; top-level and nested `toJSON`-defining bodies are redacted in the log while the caller's live object is left untouched; a private-field secret is redacted rather than leaked; an incidental moment-like `toJSON` value and a `Date` under `redactPaths` serialize to their compact `toJSON()` output rather than their internal fields; a `BigInt` body degrades gracefully via `String()`; and an end-to-end `createRequestLogger` request with a `toJSON` body + `redactPaths: ["body.password"]` redacts the log line and leaves `req.body` unmutated.
- **`redactValue` no longer leaks secrets stored on class/`Error` instances** (`src/redact.ts`, `src/types.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`, `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts`): Previously, ANY non-plain object (a class DTO, an `Error` subclass with extra properties, an ORM model) was returned AS-IS by `redactValue` without inspecting its own keys — so a secret stored as `instance.password` was written to the log line in plain text even when `"password"` was listed in `maskMetaKeys` (`createLogger`) or `maskBodyKeys` (`createRequestLogger`), because the downstream serializer (`JSON.stringify` in pretty mode, `winston.format.json()` in json mode) still enumerates the instance's own enumerable keys regardless of what `redactValue` returned. Reproduced in the core logger (both pretty and json output) and in the request-middleware body path. The fix splits non-plain objects into two cases: **pass-through built-ins** (a value with a custom `toJSON` such as `Date`/`URL`/`Buffer`/moment, an `ArrayBuffer.isView(...)` binary view, or zero enumerable own keys such as `Map`/`Set`/`RegExp`/a vanilla `Error`) continue to pass through by identity, since there is no key to redact; **data-bearing instances** (class DTOs, `Error` subclasses carrying extra enumerable properties) are now walked by their own enumerable string keys exactly like a plain object, redacting matched keys and recursing into the rest, returning the original object by identity only when nothing changed. Built-ins are also no longer added to the circular-reference `WeakSet`, incidentally fixing a second latent bug where the same `Date`/etc. referenced twice in one payload (`{ a: date, b: date }`) rendered `"[Circular]"` on the second reference. A new JSDoc "redaction boundary" note on `maskMetaKeys` / `maskBodyKeys` / `redactPaths` in `src/types.ts` documents the remaining limitation precisely: values with their own `toJSON()` are still not key-redacted (use `redactPaths` or normalize to a plain object first). New tests assert: a class instance / `Error` subclass with a masked own key is redacted (both nested-in-plain and end-to-end through `createLogger({ maskMetaKeys })` in pretty AND json mode, and through `createRequestLogger({ maskBodyKeys })` with a class-instance body); identity is preserved for every built-in and for a class instance with no matching key; a shared built-in reference renders correctly on both occurrences instead of `"[Circular]"` on the second.

### Fixed

- **`redactValue`'s cycle-detection `WeakSet` tracked every object visited anywhere in the traversal instead of only the active recursion path, so a shared (non-circular) reference collapsed to `"[Circular]"` and silently dropped real, already-redacted data from the log line** (`src/redact.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`, `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts`): The array, data-bearing-instance, and plain-object branches each added `value` to the per-call `seen` WeakSet on entry but never removed it, so `seen` accumulated as "visited anywhere in the traversal" rather than "on the current DFS path." Any object reachable from two independent points in the same payload — the identical object assigned to two sibling keys (`{ a: shared, b: shared }`), repeated in an array (`[shared, shared]`), nested at two different depths (a diamond), or (for the core logger) assigned to two different top-level metadata keys sharing one `seen` instance inside `buildMetaRedactor` — was misclassified as a cycle on its second visit and rendered as the literal string `"[Circular]"`, discarding a real, non-circular value. This reached production output through all three `redactValue` consumers: `formatMessage` (pretty-mode metadata), `buildMetaRedactor` (json-mode metadata), and `serializeBody` (request body). Fixed by converting `seen` to active-path tracking: a matching `seen.delete(value)` now runs immediately before the `return` that follows each `seen.add(value)` in the array, data-bearing-instance, and plain-object branches, so a value is only considered "in progress" while its own subtree is still being walked. A true self-cycle (`obj.self = obj`) and an indirect/mutual cycle (`a.b = b; b.a = a`) still resolve to `"[Circular]"` — the ancestor is still on the active path when its child recurses — while a diamond now renders both occurrences in full, since each sibling's subtree completes (add → delete) before the next sibling is visited. The `src/redact.ts` module docstring has been rewritten to document active-path tracking precisely. Ten new tests assert the fix end-to-end and at the unit level: `tests/logger.spec.ts` covers a shared metadata object rendering fully in both pretty mode and json mode, including a json-mode case where two different top-level metadata keys reference the same object (exercising `buildMetaRedactor`'s single shared `seen` across the whole format call); `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` covers a shared request-body object surviving `serializeBody`, plus a new `describe("redactValue active-path cycle detection (DAG/diamond fix)", ...)` block direct-testing a sibling-key diamond (masked and unmasked), a shared array entry, a two-level-deep diamond, and a shared class-DTO instance, and regression guards confirming a true self-cycle and a mutual cycle both still yield `"[Circular]"`.
- **`redactUrlQuery` corrupted a URL whose only `?` lived inside the fragment instead of leaving it unchanged** (`src/request-middleware.ts`, `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts`): The function guarded on `url.includes("?")` — checked against the WHOLE url, fragment included — but then split the pre-fragment base on `beforeFragment.indexOf("?")` with no check for a `-1` result. When the only `?` in the URL was inside the fragment (e.g. `"token=secret#?x"`), the whole-URL check passed (there IS a `?` somewhere) but `qIdx` came back `-1`, so `beforeFragment.slice(0, -1)` silently dropped the fragment-free prefix's last character and `beforeFragment.slice(0)` reparsed the mangled prefix as a bogus query string, corrupting the URL into `"token=secre?token=[REDACTED]#?x"`. A hash-router path such as `"/dash#/r?token=abc"` exercises the identical mis-slicing but happened to escape VISIBLE corruption on pre-fix code by coincidence — the mis-sliced prefix (`"/dash"`) contains no `"="`, so no pair was ever flagged as masked and the pre-existing `if (!mutated) return url;` fallback returned the original, uncorrupted string anyway. The bug never threw and never leaked a secret, but it is a genuine correctness bug — reachable by any fragment-only-`?` shape — in a helper already covered by 15+ dedicated fidelity tests. Fixed by adding `if (qIdx === -1) { return url; }` immediately after computing `qIdx`, so the whole-URL `?` check and the pre-fragment split now agree: when every `?` in the URL lives inside the fragment there is no real query component, and the URL is returned verbatim, matching the function's own documented "no query component" contract — deliberately now, rather than by the coincidental fallback described above. Three new tests assert: `redactUrlQuery("token=secret#?x", new Set(["token"]))` — the case that actually reproduces the corruption on pre-fix code — returns the input completely unchanged; `redactUrlQuery("/dash#/r?token=abc", new Set(["token"]))`, a realistic hash-router shape, also returns unchanged (kept as a post-fix guard for a realistic shape rather than as a reproduction case, since it already passed by coincidence pre-fix); and a regression guard confirms the ordinary query-before-fragment case (`"/p?token=abc#section"`) still redacts correctly to `"/p?token=[REDACTED]#section"` after the new guard.
- **`finalize()` in `createRequestLogger` now wraps all logging work in a try/catch** (`src/request-middleware.ts`): A throwing `enrich` callback, `messageBuilder`, function-form `level`, or `logger.log` call previously propagated out of the `res` `finish`/`close` event listener, causing an `uncaughtException` and a process crash — violating the package's "logging is never the cause of a request failure" invariant. The `res.removeListener(...)` calls run unconditionally before the try block so the double-fire guard always cleans up; on catch, a single bare `console.error` records the failure (mirroring `attachTransportErrorHandler` in `src/logger.ts`) without going through the logger itself (to avoid recursion). Non-Error thrown values are stringified via `String(err)`. Four new tests in `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` assert that each throw site (`enrich`, `messageBuilder`, `logger.log`, function-form `level`) produces no propagated exception and exactly one `console.error` call.
- **Function-form `level` return value is now validated at request time** (`src/request-middleware.ts`): Previously, a `level: (statusCode) => ...` callback returning an invalid string or `undefined` was passed directly to Winston, producing a `[winston] Unknown logger level` warning and silently dropping the log line — violating the package's "logging is never the cause of a request failure" invariant. The `validateRequestLevelOption` JSDoc incorrectly claimed the return was "checked at request time" when no such check existed. Now, after the callback fires inside `finalize()`, the return value is validated with `isValidLogLevel`; an invalid or `undefined` return falls back to `determineLevel(statusCode)` so the log line is always emitted at a well-formed level. The JSDoc on `validateRequestLevelOption` has been corrected to accurately describe this runtime fallback. Three new tests in `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` assert that invalid and `undefined` returns produce a logged entry at the correct status-derived level (2xx → `"info"`, 4xx → `"warn"`, 5xx → `"error"`) and that the log entry is never dropped.
- **`maxBodyLength` is now validated up front** (`src/errors.ts`, `src/request-middleware.ts`): Previously, passing `NaN` as `maxBodyLength` silently disabled the body-size guard (huge payloads were logged in full without warning), and `0` or a negative value produced an empty-preview envelope on every request — both silent misconfigurations invisible to the caller. The new `validateMaxBodyLength` helper (added to `__requestInternals`) runs at `createRequestLogger()` call time, BEFORE the `loggingEnabled`/`loggingMode` short-circuit, and throws `RequestLoggerOptionError({ code: "INVALID_BODY_LIMIT" })` for any value that is not `undefined`, a positive finite number, or `Infinity`. `Infinity` is accepted as "no limit" (body is always logged in full). The new `"INVALID_BODY_LIMIT"` code has been added to the `RequestLoggerOptionErrorCode` union in `src/errors.ts`. Six new tests in `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` assert that `NaN`, `0`, `-5`, and `"100"` (wrong type) each throw with `code === "INVALID_BODY_LIMIT"`, that a valid number passes, and that `Infinity` passes and correctly skips truncation for a 4 000-character body.
- **`redactPaths` is now validated up front** (`src/request-middleware.ts`): Previously, passing a non-array value (the natural typo `redactPaths: "body.password"` instead of `["body.password"]`) was silently ignored — the middleware fell back to `[]`, and the intended secret was never redacted. This was a latent security hole inconsistent with `maskBodyKeys`, `maskHeaderKeys`, and `maskQueryKeys`, all of which throw on the same mistake. The fix calls `validateMaskKeysOption("redactPaths", options.redactPaths, false)` at `createRequestLogger()` call time alongside the other mask-key validators, so a bare string, a non-string array entry, or any other non-array value throws `RequestLoggerOptionError({ code: "INVALID_MASK" })` immediately. `undefined` continues to be accepted (treated as `[]`). Four new tests in `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` assert: a bare string throws with `code === "INVALID_MASK"` and a message containing `"redactPaths"`; a `["a", 42]` array (non-string entry) throws with `code === "INVALID_MASK"` and a message containing both `"redactPaths"` and `"index 1"`; `undefined` does not throw; a valid `string[]` does not throw. Existing happy-path `redactPaths` tests remain green.
- **`truncateString` now runs in O(maxLength) time instead of O(input length)** (`src/request-middleware.ts`): The previous implementation used `Array.from(value).slice(0, maxLength - 1).join("") + "…"`, which forced a full O(n) walk over the entire input string to build the code-point array before slicing. For large bodies (50–100 kB) with a small `maxBodyLength` (e.g. the default 3 000 chars) this was 15–27× slower than necessary — the exact huge-payload case `maxBodyLength` exists to guard against. Replaced with a bounded `for...of` loop that breaks after collecting `maxLength - 1` code points, reducing cost to O(maxLength). Behavioral contract is identical: `for...of` over a string uses the same Unicode code-point iterator as `Array.from`, so emoji surrogate pairs (e.g. `"😀"`, U+1F600) are kept whole and a truncation boundary never lands inside a surrogate pair. All three early-return paths are preserved verbatim. Four new tests in a `describe("truncateString (Phase 6 — bounded for...of parity)", ...)` block inside `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` assert: identical output to the old `Array.from` semantics for ASCII, emoji-only, and mixed ASCII+emoji strings across multiple `maxLength` values; and a bounded-time regression guard confirming that truncating a 200 kB string to `maxLength=50` completes in under 200 ms.
- **`format: "json"` mode no longer calls `clock()` twice per log line** (`src/logger.ts`): The Console transport was being given the same `sharedFormat` object as the logger-level format. Because Winston applies the logger-level format first and then runs each transport's per-transport format on a shallow clone of the already-transformed info, `timestampCapture` (which calls the injected `clock()`) ran once at the logger level and a second time inside the Console transport — producing a different timestamp on the console JSON line than on the file JSON line. The fix gives the Console transport a separate `consoleFormat` chain (`errors + buildMetaRedactor + json`) that intentionally omits `timestampCapture`; the `info.timestamp` written by the single logger-level `clock()` call carries forward to `json()` via the clone. Three new tests in `tests/logger.spec.ts` assert: `clock()` is called exactly once per log line in json mode with a Console + Stream transport pair (counting clock); console and stream JSON lines carry the identical timestamp in json mode (fixed clock + console.log capture); pretty mode also calls `clock()` exactly once (regression guard confirming the fix did not touch the pretty branch).
- **`maskMetaKeys` is now validated up front with a structured error** (`src/errors.ts`, `src/logger.ts`): Previously, passing a bare string (the natural typo `maskMetaKeys: "password"` instead of `["password"]`), `null`, or an array with a non-string entry threw an opaque `TypeError` (e.g. `maskMetaKeys.map is not a function` or `Cannot read properties of null`), inconsistent with the structured `RequestLoggerOptionError` thrown by the parallel `maskBodyKeys` / `maskHeaderKeys` / `maskQueryKeys` options in the request-logging middleware. Now `validateMaskMetaKeysOption` runs before the logger-registry cache lookup — so bad input always throws immediately, even when a logger is already cached for the same `moduleName` + `logDirectory` combination — and emits `LoggerOptionError({ code: "INVALID_MASK" })` with a clear message naming the option and the offending type or index. The new `"INVALID_MASK"` code has been added to the `LoggerOptionErrorCode` union in `src/errors.ts`. The validator is registered in `__loggerInternals` for direct test access. Nine new tests in `tests/logger.spec.ts` assert: a bare string, `null`, and an array with a non-string entry each throw `LoggerOptionError` with `code === "INVALID_MASK"`; the throw occurs even on a cache hit; a valid `string[]` and `undefined` do not throw; the internal validator matches these semantics when called directly.
- **`formatMessage` now renders `undefined` messages as the literal `"undefined"` instead of a blank line** (`src/logger.ts`): `JSON.stringify(undefined, ...)` returns the JavaScript value `undefined` (not the string `"undefined"`), so when `info.message` was `undefined` (or a function or symbol — all values `JSON.stringify` cannot represent), `rawMessage` was `undefined` and the printf formatter emitted a blank line where the message should appear. The fix adds a `?? String(info.message)` fallback so those values are rendered via their `String()` form — `undefined` → `"undefined"`, matching how `null` → `"null"` already behaved. Two new tests in `tests/logger.spec.ts` assert: an `undefined` message renders the literal `"undefined"` in the output; a function message renders its `String()` source form rather than a blank.
- **`redactUrlQuery` rewritten to edit the raw query string in place, fixing sibling-parameter corruption and dropped protocol-relative authority** (`src/request-middleware.ts`, `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts`): The previous implementation parsed the URL with `URLSearchParams`, replaced matched values, and re-stringified via `.toString()` (with a `%5B`/`%5D` un-escape patch so the `[REDACTED]` sentinel stayed literal). That round-trip mutated bytes it should never have touched: re-serializing through `URLSearchParams` re-encodes EVERY parameter using its own encoding rules, so a non-masked sibling parameter's `%20` became `+` and `%5B`/`%5D` were silently decoded back to `[`/`]` whenever ANY parameter on the same URL was masked — the logged audit URL no longer byte-matched the real request target. Separately, reconstructing a protocol-relative URL (`//host/path?token=...`) via `${pathname}${search}${hash}` dropped the `//host` authority entirely. Both bugs share one root cause (parse-and-reserialize instead of surgical editing) and are closed by a single rewrite: the function now splits off the fragment and the pre-`?` base, splits the raw query on `&`, and for each `key=value` pair decodes only the key to test it against `maskQueryKeys` — when matched, only that pair's VALUE portion is replaced with the literal `[REDACTED]`; every other byte (non-matching pairs, sibling encoding, bare flags without `=`) is left completely untouched. The function returns the original `url` string unchanged (not just equivalent) when nothing was redacted, and the whole body is wrapped in a try/catch that returns `url` unchanged on any error, since logging must never be the cause of a request failure. The now-unnecessary `%5B`/`%5D` un-escape hack and the `URL_PARSE_SENTINEL_BASE` constant (previously used to parse relative URLs via `URL`/`URLSearchParams`) were removed entirely. New unit tests on `__requestInternals.redactUrlQuery` cover: sibling `%20` preserved when a different param is masked; sibling `%5B`/`%5D` preserved; protocol-relative `//host` authority preserved; absolute-URL host + sibling encoding preserved; repeated masked keys (`?token=a&token=b`) both redacted in order; repeated non-masked keys (`?ids=1&ids=2`) both kept verbatim; bracket array params (`?arr[]=1&arr[]=2`) kept verbatim while the sibling `token` is redacted. Existing single-param / no-mask / no-query / parse-safety expectations, and the literal `[REDACTED]` sentinel guarantee, all continue to pass.
- **`createNoopLogger()` was accidentally thenable, causing `await createNoopLogger()` to hang forever** (`src/logger.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`): The no-op logger's Proxy `get` trap had no deny-list for Promise-probe property names, so `typeof createNoopLogger().then === "function"` — any code that `await`s a no-op logger (a common pattern when a factory function is itself `async`) would hang indefinitely waiting on a Promise that never resolves, contradicting the function's own JSDoc claim of "not thenable." Fixed by checking the same `DENIED_PROXY_PROPS` deny-list the real logger's Proxy already uses (`then`/`catch`/`finally`, `$$typeof`, `nodeType`, etc.) BEFORE the catch-all unknown-method case, so those probes return `undefined` instead of a callable shim. Two related correctness gaps in the same function were fixed alongside: (1) unknown/future method calls (e.g. `createNoopLogger().someFutureMethod()`) previously returned the value-returning `noop` used internally rather than the chainable form cases 3-5 mint, so a chained call like `.someFutureMethod().info("x")` threw `TypeError: ... is not a function` — the catch-all case now returns the same chainable `(...args) => noopLogger` form; (2) `JSON.stringify(createNoopLogger())` returned the JavaScript value `undefined` (no `toJSON`), silently dropping the field when a no-op logger was nested inside a larger serialized object — a `toJSON` on the backing target now returns a stable `{ type: "@hiprax/logger", level: "silent", transports: 0 }` summary, mirroring the real logger's curated `toJSON`. New tests assert: `typeof createNoopLogger().then === "undefined"`; `await createNoopLogger()` resolves to the logger itself (raced against a real timer to prove it actually settles rather than coincidentally not hanging in a synchronous test); an unknown-method chain does not throw and returns the no-op logger; `JSON.stringify(createNoopLogger())` and `JSON.stringify({ logger: createNoopLogger() })` both produce a non-`undefined` object.
- **CommonJS TypeScript consumers under `moduleResolution: node16`/`nodenext` failed to compile with `TS1479`** (`package.json`, `tests/packaging.spec.ts`): The `"."` export only declared a single top-level `types: "./dist/index.d.ts"` (the ESM declaration). TypeScript's `node16`/`nodenext` resolution modes select the `types` condition matching the consumer's actual module system — a `require("@hiprax/logger")` consumer therefore matched the ESM-shaped `.d.ts` and failed with `TS1479` ("the current file is a CommonJS module... cannot be imported"), even though `tsup` was already emitting a correct `dist/index.d.cts` CommonJS declaration that nothing referenced. Reproduced against the project's own `tsc`. Fixed by replacing the bare `types` field with per-condition declarations: `import` resolves `{ types: "./dist/index.d.ts", default: "./dist/index.mjs" }` and `require` resolves `{ types: "./dist/index.d.cts", default: "./dist/index.cjs" }`. Runtime resolution (the `default` targets) is unchanged for both ESM and CJS consumers; the legacy top-level `main`/`module`/`types` fields are kept for consumers on older `moduleResolution` settings (`"node"`, `"bundler"`). A new `tests/packaging.spec.ts` reads `package.json` and asserts the `"."` export's `import.types`/`require.types`/`import.default`/`require.default` values, and that both declaration files exist on disk after `npm run build` — pinning the mapping so a future edit cannot silently re-break CJS TypeScript consumers.
- **`rotation.maxFiles: "14D"` (uppercase day suffix) silently behaved as 14 FILES instead of the documented 14 DAYS** (`src/logger.ts`, `src/types.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`): `MAX_FILES_PATTERN` (`/^\d+d?$/i`) and the `RotationStrategy.maxFiles` JSDoc both advertised the day suffix as case-insensitive, but the upstream `file-stream-rotator` parser that `winston-daily-rotate-file` delegates to checks the suffix case-SENSITIVELY (`max_logs.toString().substr(-1) === 'd'`), confirmed against the installed `node_modules/file-stream-rotator/FileStreamRotator.js`. A logger configured with `maxFiles: "14D"` therefore validated successfully and was documented as 14-day retention, but silently fell through to upstream's file-COUNT branch instead (kept as 14 files, never pruned by age). Fixed with a new `normalizeMaxFiles()` helper that lowercases a string `maxFiles` value before it reaches `DailyRotateFile`, applied both in `buildRotateTransport` (the real transport) and when building the resolved rotation objects that feed `buildOptionsSignature` (so `"14d"` and `"14D"` hash to the same registry signature). Normalizing rather than rejecting uppercase `D` keeps every previously-accepted value working — fully backward compatible — and mirrors how upstream already lowercases the sibling `maxSize` suffix internally. A new test asserts a logger built with `rotation: { maxFiles: "14D" }` hands the resolved transport `"14d"` (introspected directly), i.e. is genuinely treated as 14 days.
- **A 2nd `createLogger()` call with a different `maskMetaKeys` on an already-cached key silently returned the cached logger with NO warning** (`src/logger.ts`, `tests/logger.spec.ts`): `buildOptionsSignature` — the canonical signature used to detect and warn about conflicting `createLogger()` calls on the same `moduleName` + `logDirectory` — omitted `maskMetaKeys`, `colorize`, and `captureUncaught`. Because `maskMetaKeys` is security-relevant, a second call requesting a DIFFERENT redaction list than the cached logger was already using silently kept the old (possibly empty, possibly different) redaction config with no signal that the requested change was ignored — a caller could reasonably believe their secrets were now being masked when they were not. Fixed by adding `maskMetaKeys` (lowercased + sorted, order-independent), the _resolved_ `colorize` flags (`resolveColorizeFlags(...)`, computed before the cache lookup so a divergent `colorize` is also caught), and `captureUncaught` to the signature, so a divergent 2nd call now fires the existing one-time `console.warn` naming the divergent fields, same as it already did for `level`/`rotation`/etc. New tests assert: two `createLogger()` calls on the same key differing only in `maskMetaKeys` emit exactly one conflict warning naming `maskMetaKeys`; the internal `buildOptionsSignature` includes the new fields and is order-independent for `maskMetaKeys`; an identical 2nd call (including identical `maskMetaKeys` in a different array order) still does not warn.
- **`shutdownLogger` no longer caches a timed-out (rejected) promise** (`src/logger.ts`): Previously, the internal `WeakMap<winston.Logger, Promise<void>>` stored the shutdown promise regardless of whether it resolved or rejected. A timed-out shutdown left a cached rejected promise so any subsequent `shutdownLogger(logger, { timeoutMs: longer })` call immediately returned the old rejection — the retry was silently impossible. Now a `.catch()` handler placed before `.finally()` evicts the WeakMap entry on rejection (guarded by an identity check to avoid accidentally evicting a concurrently-installed retry promise), so a later call can start a fresh flush attempt. Successful shutdowns are unaffected — their cached resolved promise is the correct idempotent "already done" signal and is never evicted. The `shutdownLogger` JSDoc has been updated to document the distinction: successful shutdowns are idempotent (same promise, `logger.end()` called once); timed-out shutdowns are retryable (WeakMap evicted, fresh attempt on the next call). Three new tests in `tests/logger.spec.ts` assert: (a) after a timeout-rejection the second call returns a different promise and resolves when the transport eventually emits `finish`; (b) a successful shutdown is idempotent — a second and third call return the same resolved promise and `logger.end()` is called exactly once; (c) three concurrent same-tick calls share one in-flight promise and `logger.end()` is called exactly once.

### Added

- **`ShutdownOptions` is now a public export** (`src/logger.ts`, `src/index.ts`): The `ShutdownOptions` interface (previously defined but unexported in `src/logger.ts`) is now exported from both `src/logger.ts` and re-exported as a type from `src/index.ts`. Consumers who call `shutdownLogger(logger, opts)` or `shutdownAllLoggers(opts)` can now explicitly type the options variable (`const opts: ShutdownOptions = { timeoutMs: 5000 }`) without an `as` cast or inlining the shape. Additive and fully backward-compatible — no behavior changes. Two new tests in `tests/logger.spec.ts` assert: the type is importable and usable as a type annotation; and the optional `timeoutMs` field resolves to `undefined` when omitted.

### Changed

- **`release.yml` publish step now uses `--ignore-scripts`** (`.github/workflows/release.yml`): The release workflow ran an explicit `Build` step and `npm pack --dry-run` to verify the `dist/` artifacts, but the publish step invoked `npm publish --provenance --access public` which triggers the `prepublishOnly` lifecycle hook and rebuilds `dist/` a second time before publishing — meaning the verified build was discarded and an unverified rebuilt copy was uploaded to npm. Changed to `npm publish --provenance --access public --ignore-scripts` so the exact `dist/` artifacts verified by the build and dry-run steps are the artifacts that are signed with npm provenance and published. `prepublishOnly` still protects manual/local publishes that do not pass `--ignore-scripts`.
- **GitHub Actions bumped to latest verified stable majors** (`.github/workflows/release.yml`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `.github/workflows/codeql.yml`): Bumped `actions/checkout` from `@v6` to `@v7` across all three workflow files (v7.0.0 released, `@v7` major float tag confirmed stable via GitHub API). Bumped `codecov/codecov-action` from `@v6` to `@v7` in `ci.yml` (v7.0.0 released, `@v7` major float tag confirmed). Actions left at their current major where already on the latest stable: `actions/setup-node` (`@v6`), `actions/upload-artifact` (`@v7`), `softprops/action-gh-release` (`@v3`), `github/codeql-action` (`@v4`).

- **`enrich` return type widened to `| null | undefined`** (`src/types.ts`): The `enrich` callback in `RequestLoggerOptions` was typed as `=> Record<string, unknown>`, but the runtime always applied `?? undefined` to collapse a `null` or `undefined` return before assigning `entry.context`. Consumers who legitimately return `null` or `undefined` from `enrich` (for example, a conditional enricher that returns `null` when there is no extra context to attach) had to work around the narrow type with `as unknown as Record<string, unknown>`. The return type is now `=> Record<string, unknown> | null | undefined`, matching the documented and already-handled runtime contract. No behavior changes. The two existing tests that used the `as unknown` cast have been updated to use the direct return type. Two new type-level tests in `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` assert that `() => null` and `() => undefined` are accepted without a cast.
- **`maskBodyKeys` is now pre-resolved to a `Set` at middleware construction time** (`src/request-middleware.ts`): Previously, `serializeBody` was called with the raw `maskBodyKeys` string array on every request, building a new lowercased `Set` per request — inconsistent with `maskHeaderKeys` and `maskQueryKeys`, which have been pre-resolved to `ReadonlySet<string>` at construction time since Phase 4. The fix pre-resolves `bodyMaskSet: ReadonlySet<string> | undefined` once at `createRequestLogger()` call time (alongside `headerMaskSet` and `queryMaskSet`) and passes it to `serializeBody` in the per-request `finalize()` closure. `serializeBody` is widened to accept `string[] | ReadonlySet<string>` for `maskKeys` so the construction-time path passes a Set directly (zero per-request allocation) while the `__requestInternals.serializeBody` test-direct path continues to accept plain arrays for backward compatibility. No behavior changes. Five new tests assert: end-to-end body masking via the pre-resolved path (case-insensitive, via `createRequestLogger`); `serializeBody` accepting a `ReadonlySet` and redacting correctly; `serializeBody` continuing to accept a plain `string[]` array.

### Tests

- **`shutdownAllLoggers` partial-timeout rejection and per-logger independence** (`tests/logger.spec.ts`): The only existing coverage for `shutdownAllLoggers()` was the all-healthy `.resolves` case — the documented "rejects on any single logger's timeout, independently of the others" contract (a `Promise.all` over per-logger `shutdownLogger()` calls) had no regression guard, so a future refactor to `Promise.allSettled` could silently flip that contract while staying green at 100% coverage. New test registers one healthy logger and one whose only transport stalls in `_final` (reusing the existing `StalledTransport` pattern), asserts `shutdownAllLoggers({ timeoutMs: 50 })` rejects with `/shutdownLogger timed out after 50ms/`, and then confirms independence by immediately calling `shutdownLogger(healthy)` and observing it resolves from the already-cached `WeakMap` entry — proving the healthy logger had already flushed on its own, unaffected by the other logger's timeout.
- **JSON-mode circular-reference handling, end-to-end** (`tests/logger.spec.ts`): Circular references were previously only asserted in pretty mode and via direct `redactValue` unit calls — neither json-mode code path (`buildMetaRedactor`'s `WeakSet`-based cycle detection when `maskMetaKeys` is configured, or `winston.format.json()`'s own safe-stable-stringify when it is not) had an end-to-end regression guard. New tests log a self-referential metadata object (`const c = { keep: 1 }; c.self = c;`) once WITH `maskMetaKeys` configured and once WITHOUT, parse each emitted line as JSON, and assert both are valid JSON containing the literal `"[Circular]"` with no thrown exception.

### Documentation

- **README and project reference docs updated for this release's behavior changes** (`README.md`, `CLAUDE.md`): Added a "TypeScript Module Resolution" section to `README.md` explaining the new per-condition `exports.types` mapping (see the CJS-types fix above) and a redaction-boundary caveat to the `maskMetaKeys` / `maskBodyKeys` option descriptions and the Security Notes section (values with a custom `toJSON()`, or zero enumerable keys, are not key-redacted). `CLAUDE.md` was updated to describe: the new leak-safe `redactValue` contract (pass-through built-ins vs. walked data-bearing instances); the rewritten in-place `redactUrlQuery`; the `exports` map's per-condition `types` shape; the `maxFiles` case-insensitivity fix (`normalizeMaxFiles`); the expanded `buildOptionsSignature` field list; the `createNoopLogger` not-thenable / chainable / `toJSON` contract; and the updated test count (304 → 346 across three spec files, with the new `tests/packaging.spec.ts` now listed in the architecture tree).
- **`maskQueryKeys` documentation corrected to match the in-place `redactUrlQuery` rewrite** (`README.md`, `src/types.ts`): Two doc sites still described the pre-rewrite implementation that parsed the URL with `URLSearchParams` and re-stringified via `.toString()` — the exact round-trip that the `redactUrlQuery` rewrite (see the Fixed entry above) deleted because it corrupted sibling-parameter encoding (`%20` → `+`) and dropped the `//host` authority of protocol-relative URLs. The README "Security Notes" `maskQueryKeys` bullet claimed it "parses ... with `URLSearchParams` ... and re-stringifies", and the `RequestLoggerOptions.maskQueryKeys` JSDoc claimed "Re-stringification preserves the original parameter order and uses URL-encoded form." Both now accurately describe the current behavior: the raw query string is edited in place, only a matched parameter's value is replaced with the literal `[REDACTED]`, and every other byte (parameter order, sibling-param percent-encoding, the `//host` authority, and the fragment) is preserved exactly — no parsing, re-encoding, or re-serialization occurs.

## [0.21.9] - 2026-06-30

### Security / Dependencies (2026-06-30)

- **Closed all three open Dependabot alerts** by pinning the affected transitive **devDependencies** to their patched versions via a new top-level `overrides` block in `package.json`. All three are build/test-time-only transitive dependencies — none is a runtime dependency and none ships in the published tarball (the `files` allowlist is `dist`, `README.md`, `LICENSE`), so consumers of `@hiprax/logger` were never exposed; the alerts only ever concerned the maintainer's CI and local dev toolchain. `npm audit` now reports **0 vulnerabilities at every audit level** (was 1 moderate + 2 low). Full pre-completion checklist passes (build, 253/253 tests + 100% coverage on every metric, lint, type-check, format:check).
  - **`js-yaml` → `^3.15.0` (override `"js-yaml@3"`)** — closes **GHSA-h67p-54hq-rp68 / CVE-2026-53550** (moderate; quadratic-complexity DoS in YAML merge-key handling via repeated aliases). Reached only through `ts-jest > @jest/transform > babel-plugin-istanbul > @istanbuljs/load-nyc-config`, which parses the project's own trusted nyc/istanbul config during the coverage step of `npm test`; the package never parses untrusted YAML. The `@3` selector key scopes the override to the js-yaml **3.x line only** so a future 4.x consumer is never forced down to 3.x (verified against npm 11's version-range override semantics).
  - **`@babel/core` → `^7.29.7`** — closes **GHSA-4x5r-pxfx-6jf8 / CVE-2026-49356** (low; arbitrary file read via crafted `//# sourceMappingURL=` comment, CWE-22). Pulled in only through jest / ts-jest's transform plumbing, where Babel only ever processes the project's own first-party source; the advisory itself notes that "users that only compile trusted code are not impacted."
  - **`esbuild` → `^0.28.1`** — closes **GHSA-g7r4-m6w7-qqqr** (low; Windows-only arbitrary file read in esbuild's HTTP development server, CWE-22). Reached only through `tsup` for one-shot bundling; esbuild's dev server (`serve` / `--servedir`) is never started by this package, so there is no request surface to traverse. `tsup@8.5.1` (the latest release) still declares `esbuild ^0.27.0`, so an `overrides` pin is the only way to reach the fix while keeping `tsup` on its latest version; the build and full test suite confirm `tsup@8.5.1` is fully compatible with `esbuild@0.28.1` (dual ESM/CJS + declaration output unchanged). `package-lock.json` updated to the patched resolutions (`js-yaml@3.15.0`, `@babel/core@7.29.7`, `esbuild@0.28.1` plus its platform-specific `@esbuild/*` packages); `npm ci` confirmed in sync.

### Packaging (2026-06-30)

- **Stopped publishing the raw `src/*.ts` sources** (`package.json` `files`, `.npmignore`). Removed `src` from the `files` allowlist, trimming the published tarball from **15 files to 9** (`LICENSE`, `README.md`, `package.json`, and the six `dist/` artifacts) and the unpacked size from ~558 kB to ~430 kB. The raw sources were redundant for debugging: the `dist/*.map` sourcemaps already inline the complete original TypeScript via `sourcesContent` (verified), so debuggers resolve original source from the maps alone, and no declaration maps (`.d.ts.map`) are emitted — meaning a shipped `src/` would not have powered "go to definition" from the type declarations either. The package's only public entry is the `.` export (there is no `./src` subpath in the `exports` map), so omitting the sources breaks **no** consumer import path; this is a packaging-only change with no API or behavioral impact. The `.npmignore` header and a new defensive `src/` entry were updated to document the new published set and keep the sources out even if the allowlist is ever broadened.

## [0.21.8] - 2026-06-30

### Packaging (2026-06-30)

- **Added `.npmignore`** (`.npmignore`): a defensive second layer over the existing `package.json` `files` allowlist (`dist`, `src`, `README.md`, `LICENSE`), which remains the primary control over what gets published. The published tarball is unchanged at 15 files (`LICENSE`, `README.md`, `package.json`, the six `dist/` artifacts including sourcemaps, and the six `src/*.ts` sources); `npm pack --dry-run` confirms no test, config, script, CI, or local tooling file is included. Because the `files` allowlist force-includes the `dist` and `src` directories, the ignore file does not change current contents; it documents what must never ship and keeps stray cruft (test files, coverage, caches, OS / editor files, env files, logs, local `*.tmp` backups, packed `*.tgz`, and the local orchestrator tooling) out of the tarball even if the allowlist is ever broadened or removed. The `.ts` sources and `.map` sourcemaps are intentionally retained so consumers can resolve original source while debugging.
- **`.gitignore`** (`.gitignore`): added a dedicated "Orchestrator" section so local orchestrator tooling never enters version control (`.orchestrator/`, `orchestrator/`, `.orchestrator.ps1`, `.orchestrator.env`, `orchestrator.ps1`, `orchestrator.env`, `phases.json`, `PLAN.md`, `plan.md`).

## [0.21.7] - 2026-05-12

### Documentation (2026-05-12)

- **`README.md` badge row trimmed.** Removed the `Dependencies` badge (Libraries.io release status for `@hiprax/logger`) from the header row. The remaining badges retain their existing order: npm version → npm provenance → License → CI → CodeQL → codecov → Node.js.

### Security / Code Scanning (2026-05-12)

- **Closed all three open CodeQL `security-and-quality` alerts on the repo.** The CodeQL scan that runs on push to `main`, on PRs targeting `main`, and on a weekly schedule was reporting three open findings in the Security tab. None had a known runtime impact (each was either statically mitigated by surrounding code or operated on a narrowly trusted input), but all three were silenced at the source so the Security tab is now clean. Full pre-completion checklist passes (build, 253/253 tests + 100% coverage on every metric, lint, type-check, format:check). `npm audit` continues to report 0 vulnerabilities at every audit level.
  - **`js/polynomial-redos` — HIGH severity — `src/logger.ts:324` (alert #3).** The trailing-hyphen anchor `[-]+$` inside `sanitizeSegment` matched CodeQL's canonical polynomial-regex shape (a quantified, anchored repetition that backtracks across every candidate start position when the string does NOT end with the quantified character). The runtime exposure was theoretical — the preceding `.replace(/-+/g, "-")` already collapses every run of `-` to a single character, so the anchored regex only ever sees a string with no adjacent hyphens and cannot exhibit the polynomial behavior — but CodeQL's local taint flow does not see that contextual mitigation. Replaced `^[-]+|[-]+$` with `^-|-$` (no `+` quantifier), which is provably linear on any input and semantically equivalent here. The fix is paired with a new bounded-time regression test (`tests/logger.spec.ts:2427`) that feeds a 100,000-character hostile input through `sanitizeSegment` and asserts the call finishes in under 1 s — a hard ceiling that any future regression to a quadratic-shaped regex would blow past. CWE-1333, CWE-400, CWE-730.
  - **`js/prototype-polluting-assignment` — MEDIUM severity — `src/request-middleware.ts:449` (alert #2).** The bracket assignment `target[finalKey] = REDACTED` at the bottom of `redactEntryPath` was the surface CodeQL flagged. The function ALREADY had a deny-list guard that ran over every segment of the user-supplied `redactPaths` path before any traversal, returning early if any segment matched `__proto__` / `constructor` / `prototype`. With that guard in place the bracket assignment was safe in practice, but the single upstream loop checked all segments together — including the final one — so CodeQL's local taint flow could not statically prove that `finalKey` was never a forbidden key by the time it reached the assignment. Restructured the guard into two stages that protect distinct concerns: part 1 — the upstream loop now iterates `segments[0..length-2]` (intermediate segments that get traversed via `cursor[segments[i]]`, where a forbidden key would land the cursor on `Object.prototype` itself); part 2 — a new inline check immediately before the assignment that gates the final segment. Both stages reference the existing `FORBIDDEN_OBJECT_KEYS` set (single source of truth across the package — header accumulators, `redactValue` rebuild, and now `redactEntryPath`). Behavior is unchanged: every prototype-pollution path that returned early before still returns early now, just from a different (and CodeQL-visible) statement. Added a focused regression test (`tests/request-middleware.spec.ts:1577`) that mounts a `JSON.parse('{"__proto__": "real"}')` object as the assignment cursor and walks a path whose ONLY forbidden segment is the final one — the harder case where the upstream `hasOwnProperty` filter would NOT short-circuit on its own, so the new inline guard is the load-bearing check. CWE-1321, CWE-78, CWE-79, CWE-94, CWE-471, CWE-915.
  - **`js/incomplete-sanitization` — HIGH severity — `scripts/release-tag.mjs:88` (alert #1).** The CHANGELOG excerpt extractor built its regex from `pkg.version.replace(/\./g, "\\.")` — a hand-rolled escape that handled only `.` and left `\`, `+`, `*`, `?`, `^`, `$`, `{`, `}`, `(`, `)`, `|`, `[`, `]` un-escaped. The runtime exposure is essentially zero because `pkg.version` is read from `package.json` and is always a plain SemVer string (the upstream `release:prepare` script that mutates it runs `npm version`, which validates the input), but a hand-edited or compromised `package.json` with a metacharacter in the `version` field would produce a malformed regex or one that matched more than intended. Replaced the single-char escape with the standard MDN-documented regex-metachar escape `s.replace(/[.*+?^${}()|[\]\\]/g, "\\$&")`. The release-tag script is a Node CLI with no surrounding test harness, so the CodeQL alert closing on the next scan is the verification signal. CWE-20, CWE-80, CWE-116.

### Tests (2026-05-12)

- **`tests/logger.spec.ts` — new `sanitizeSegment` bounded-time test.** `it("sanitizeSegment handles long runs of hyphens in linear time (ReDoS regression)")` pins the linear-time invariant against a 100,000-character hostile input. Asserts both the time bound (`< 1000 ms`) and the behavioral invariant (`"x"` survives, the leading hyphen run is stripped). Lives inside the existing `property-based — sanitizeSegment / buildLogFilePath` describe block.
- **`tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` — new `redactEntryPath` final-segment guard test.** `it("redactEntryPath final-segment guard catches __proto__ as an OWN property on the cursor")` exercises the new part-2 guard with the harder JSON-parsed-`__proto__` case where the `hasOwnProperty` filter would NOT short-circuit on its own. Asserts the own `__proto__` key on the cursor is left untouched, the cursor's prototype chain is unchanged, and `Object.prototype` is unpolluted globally. Lives inside the existing `prototype-pollution hardening` describe block.
- Test count: 251 → **253** (+2). Coverage remains 100% on statements, branches, functions, and lines across `errors.ts`, `logger.ts`, `redact.ts`, and `request-middleware.ts`.

## [0.21.6] - 2026-05-12

### CI / Workflow Hardening (2026-05-12)

- **GitHub Actions versions refreshed to current latest majors across all workflows** (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `.github/workflows/release.yml`, `.github/workflows/codeql.yml`). All bumps are Node 24 runtime refreshes with no input/output schema changes — the workflows continue to consume identical YAML inputs and produce identical artifacts, status checks, and release outputs. GitHub-hosted runners have shipped Node 24 support since runner `v2.327.1` (Aug 2025), so the matrix legs (Ubuntu / Windows × Node 18 / 20 / 22 / 24) and the release job all continue to schedule on first-attempt with no runner pinning required. The full pre-completion checklist passes (build, 251/251 tests + 100% coverage on every metric, lint, type-check, format:check) and `npm audit` continues to report 0 vulnerabilities at every audit level. Runtime + dev npm dependencies are already pinned to their respective `latest` dist-tags as of the prior 0.21.5 cut — no `package.json` dep movement in this release; the `package-lock.json` change is the version-line bump only.
  - `actions/checkout`: `v4` → `v6` (runtime: Node 20 → Node 24; credential persistence moved to a separate file in `v6.0.0`).
  - `actions/setup-node`: `v4` → `v6` (runtime: Node 20 → Node 24; `v5` introduced opt-in `packageManager`-field auto-detection but we already pass an explicit `cache: "npm"` input, so behavior is unchanged; `v6` narrowed the auto-detect to npm only).
